title: Room Temperature Sensor Placement

components.name: Temperature sensor mounted at 5 feet on interior wall
Sensor located directly above a supply air diffuser
components.violation: Temperature sensors must not be placed in direct airflow from supply diffusers. The supply air temperature causes false readings, making the controller unable to maintain proper space temperature setpoint.

components.name: Sensor wired with shielded plenum-rated cable
Sensor mounted on an exterior wall next to a window
components.violation: Sensors on exterior walls are influenced by solar gain and outdoor temperature conducted through the wall, producing inaccurate space readings. Mount sensors on interior walls per ASHRAE guidelines.

components.name: Sensor connected to correct DDC controller input
Sensor cover plate installed flush to wall

explanation: Proper sensor placement is foundational to accurate building controls. Sensors near supply diffusers or on exterior walls give false readings, causing energy waste and comfort complaints. ASHRAE Guideline 36 specifies placement on interior walls, away from direct airflow.

title: DDC Controller Wiring Panel

components.name: Controller mounted in NEMA 1 enclosure
24VAC power supply wired correctly
Sensor wiring run in same conduit as 120VAC power
components.violation: Low-voltage signal wiring (Class 2) must be separated from line-voltage power wiring. Running them together induces electromagnetic interference that corrupts sensor readings and can damage controller inputs.

components.name: All field wiring labeled at terminal strips
Analog output wired to binary input terminal
components.violation: Connecting a 0-10VDC analog output signal to a binary (digital) input terminal will not control the device properly. Binary inputs only read on/off states. Analog signals must connect to analog input terminals for proportional control.

components.name: End-of-line resistor installed on supervised input

explanation: DDC controller wiring requires careful attention to signal separation per NEC and correct terminal assignments per the controller's I/O map. Mixing signal types or running low-voltage with line-voltage are common apprentice mistakes.

title: BACnet Network Wiring

components.name: MS/TP trunk cable using 22 AWG shielded twisted pair
Daisy-chain topology from controller to controller
Star topology with home runs to a central hub
components.violation: BACnet MS/TP requires a daisy-chain (bus) topology, not star topology. Star wiring creates signal reflections and communication failures. Each device must connect in series on the bus.

components.name: Network shield grounded at both ends of the trunk
components.violation: The shield on a BACnet MS/TP trunk must be grounded at one end only to prevent ground loops. Grounding at both ends creates circulating currents that introduce noise and cause intermittent communication failures.

components.name: End-of-line termination resistor installed

explanation: BACnet MS/TP is an RS-485 based network that requires proper bus topology, single-point shield grounding, and end-of-line termination. Incorrect wiring is the leading cause of BAS communication problems.

title: Thermostat Installation in Office

components.name: Programmable thermostat at 4.5 feet height
Thermostat mounted on a wall shared with a kitchen
components.violation: Mounting a thermostat on a wall adjacent to a heat-producing space (kitchen, server room, boiler room) causes conducted heat to affect readings. Sensors must be on walls away from abnormal heat sources.

components.name: Setpoint adjustment limited to +/- 3 degrees F
Thermostat located behind an open door
components.violation: A thermostat behind a door that is typically held open will be in stagnant air and will not sense the true space temperature. It must be in an area with representative airflow of the occupied zone.

components.name: Wiring connected per color code on controller diagram

explanation: Thermostat and space sensor location dramatically affects comfort and energy use. Even a correctly calibrated sensor in the wrong location produces poor control. ASHRAE specifies interior walls with representative air circulation.

title: Control Valve Actuator Setup

components.name: Two-way control valve sized to match coil flow
Valve actuator installed on the valve body
Actuator spring return set to fail-open on a cooling coil
components.violation: Cooling coil control valves should fail closed (spring return to closed) on loss of signal or power. Failing open causes uncontrolled cooling and potential freezing of downstream coils or spaces.

components.name: Valve installed with flow arrow matching piping direction
Actuator 0-10VDC signal wired to analog output
Valve body installed on the return side of the coil
components.violation: Control valves should be installed on the supply (entering) side of the coil for proper authority and control. Return-side installation reduces valve authority and causes sluggish temperature response.
components.codeReference: ASHRAE Handbook — HVAC Systems and Equipment

explanation: Control valve installation requires attention to fail-safe position and piping location. Cooling valves fail closed, heating valves fail open. Valve placement on the supply side ensures better control authority per ASHRAE standards.

title: AHU Economizer Control Sequence

components.name: Outdoor air temperature sensor installed in weather-protected housing
Mixed air temperature sensor downstream of mixing plenum
Economizer enabled based on outdoor dry-bulb temperature only
components.violation: In humid climates, dry-bulb economizer control can introduce excessive moisture. ASHRAE 90.1 and Guideline 36 require differential enthalpy or fixed-enthalpy control for climate zones where humidity is a factor.

components.name: Minimum outdoor air damper position set to 0% during unoccupied
components.violation: Minimum outdoor air position must meet ventilation requirements per ASHRAE 62.1 during occupied modes. Setting to 0% is acceptable during unoccupied periods only if the sequence properly distinguishes occupied vs. unoccupied modes and ensures pre-occupancy purge.

components.name: Return air CO2 sensor for demand-controlled ventilation
Outdoor air damper actuator with spring-return to minimum

explanation: Economizer control is one of the most impactful energy-saving sequences. Using dry-bulb only in humid climates wastes energy by bringing in moisture that must be removed. Proper occupied/unoccupied ventilation switching is essential for energy code compliance.

title: VAV Box Controller Configuration

components.name: Zone temperature sensor connected to controller
Airflow sensor ring calibrated for velocity range
Minimum airflow setpoint set to 0 CFM
components.violation: VAV box minimum airflow must never be 0 CFM in occupied mode. ASHRAE 62.1 requires minimum ventilation airflow to each zone at all times during occupancy. Setting minimum to 0 causes ventilation code violation and poor indoor air quality.

components.name: Damper actuator mechanically linked to damper blade
Heating setpoint programmed 5 degrees below cooling setpoint
components.violation: A 5-degree deadband between heating and cooling setpoints is too wide for most comfort standards but the issue is this is below the ASHRAE 55 recommended range. Per ASHRAE Guideline 36, the typical deadband should be 2-4 degrees F to prevent simultaneous heating and cooling while maintaining comfort.

components.name: Hot water reheat valve connected to controller output

explanation: VAV controller configuration directly affects comfort and energy. Zero minimum airflow violates ventilation code, and excessive deadband causes occupant complaints. ASHRAE Guideline 36 provides standard sequences for VAV zone control.

title: BACnet Device Addressing

components.name: Each controller assigned a unique MAC address on the MS/TP trunk
Two devices on different trunks sharing the same device instance number
components.violation: BACnet device instance numbers must be globally unique across the entire BACnet internetwork, not just per trunk. Duplicate device instances cause routing conflicts and make one or both devices unreachable from the operator workstation.

components.name: MS/TP MAC addresses set between 0 and 127
BACnet router configured between MS/TP and IP networks
Network number set to 0 on a routed network segment
components.violation: Network number 0 is reserved and means 'local network only' in BACnet. A routed segment must have a unique non-zero network number (1-65534) assigned. Using 0 prevents the router from forwarding messages to and from this segment.

components.name: Device object name set descriptively (e.g., AHU-1-Controller)

explanation: BACnet addressing errors are one of the most common commissioning issues. Device instances must be globally unique, and network numbers must be non-zero on routed segments. These rules are defined in ASHRAE 135 and must be coordinated across the entire building.

title: Alarm Configuration for AHU

components.name: High discharge air temperature alarm at 130 degrees F
Low mixed air temperature alarm at 40 degrees F
Filter differential pressure alarm configured
All alarms set to the same priority level
components.violation: Alarms must be prioritized based on severity. A freeze stat alarm (life-safety) should be higher priority than a dirty filter alarm (maintenance). Without prioritization, critical alarms get lost in nuisance alarms, a condition called 'alarm flooding'.

components.name: Freeze stat alarm with no automatic equipment shutdown
components.violation: A freeze stat alarm must automatically shut down the AHU fan, close the outdoor air damper, and open the heating valve. An alarm-only response without automatic action risks coil freeze-up and catastrophic water damage.

components.name: Supply fan status feedback configured

explanation: Alarm management is critical for building operations. ASHRAE Guideline 13 addresses alarm prioritization, and Guideline 36 requires automatic protective actions for critical conditions like freeze protection. Poorly configured alarms lead to ignored warnings and equipment damage.

title: Trending and Data Logging Setup

components.name: Key analog points trended at 15-minute intervals
Trend logs stored only in the field controller memory
components.violation: Storing trend data only in field controllers risks data loss when controllers are reset or lose power. Trend data should be archived to the BAS server or a dedicated historian. Controller memory is limited and will overwrite oldest data.

components.name: Binary status points (fan on/off) trended on change-of-value
Trend data resolution set to whole numbers only for temperatures
components.violation: Temperature trend data should have at least 0.1 degree resolution. Whole-number-only resolution (e.g., 72 vs. 72.3) loses critical information needed for analyzing control loop performance, setpoint drift, and energy optimization.

components.name: Outdoor air temperature and humidity trended for energy analysis

explanation: Trend data is essential for commissioning, fault detection, and energy management. Data must be archived off the controller, and resolution must be sufficient for meaningful analysis. ASHRAE Guideline 13 provides data management best practices.

title: Chiller Plant Staging Sequence

components.name: Lead chiller starts on rising chilled water return temperature
Chilled water supply temperature reset based on outdoor air
Lag chiller stages on immediately when lead reaches 100% capacity
components.violation: Staging a lag chiller immediately at 100% lead capacity causes hunting and instability. A time delay (typically 10-15 minutes) and sustained load confirmation are required to prevent short-cycling. ASHRAE Guideline 36 specifies staging criteria with time delays and load verification.

components.name: Condenser water pumps and tower fans start before chiller
No minimum runtime enforced between chiller starts
components.violation: Chillers require a minimum runtime (typically 15-30 minutes) and minimum off-time (typically 15 minutes) to prevent compressor short-cycling. Frequent starts damage the compressor motor and reduce equipment life.

components.name: Chiller isolation valves open before chiller enable

explanation: Chiller plant staging is among the most complex control sequences. Proper time delays, minimum runtimes, and load verification prevent compressor damage and optimize energy efficiency. ASHRAE Guideline 36 provides standardized chiller plant sequences.

title: Smoke Control System Integration

components.name: Smoke detectors hardwired to fire alarm panel
BAS receives fire alarm signal via network interface
BAS software controls smoke damper positions during fire event
components.violation: Smoke control and fire safety functions must NOT rely on BAS software for execution. Smoke dampers must be directly controlled by the fire alarm panel through hardwired connections. BAS can monitor status but cannot be the primary controller for life-safety functions.

components.name: Stairwell pressurization fan starts on fire alarm
AHU shutdown on duct smoke detector uses BAS command only
components.violation: Duct smoke detector shutdown must be hardwired directly to the AHU disconnect or starter, independent of the BAS. A BAS software command alone is not acceptable for life-safety shutdown because software can fail or be overridden.

components.name: Smoke control status displayed on BAS operator workstation

explanation: Fire and smoke control integration is the most critical controls application. Life-safety functions must be hardwired and independent of BAS software. The BAS can monitor and display status but must never be the sole means of controlling life-safety equipment per NFPA 92 and IBC 909.

title: Union Hall Training: BACnet Network Troubleshooting

components.name: Shielded twisted pair cable used for MS/TP trunk
Consistent baud rate (76800) set on all devices
No end-of-line termination resistor on last device in the daisy chain
components.violation: Every BACnet MS/TP trunk requires a 120-ohm termination resistor at both physical ends of the bus. Missing the end-of-line resistor causes signal reflections that corrupt data frames, leading to intermittent communication failures and token-passing errors across the entire trunk.
components.codeReference: ASHRAE 135 (BACnet) — Annex J, MS/TP Physical Layer

components.name: Daisy-chain wiring has a stub/tee connection to a branch device
components.violation: BACnet MS/TP requires a strict daisy-chain (bus) topology with no stubs or tees. A stub creates an impedance mismatch that causes signal reflections, corrupting communications for all devices on the trunk. Branch devices must be wired in-line on the bus, not teed off.

components.name: Device address 127 assigned to a field controller
components.violation: MAC address 127 is reserved for broadcast messages on BACnet MS/TP networks. Assigning address 127 to a device causes it to interpret all broadcast traffic as addressed to itself, disrupting token passing and network communication for every device on the trunk.

explanation: BACnet MS/TP troubleshooting is a core IBEW controls apprentice skill taught at the union hall. The three most common wiring mistakes — missing termination, stub/tee branches, and reserved address usage — account for the majority of field communication problems. Proper RS-485 bus discipline (termination at both ends, daisy-chain only, valid MAC addresses 0-126) is essential for reliable building automation networks.

title: Union Hall Training: Pneumatic-to-DDC Conversion

components.name: New DDC controller mounted in NEMA 1 enclosure
Proper labeling on all points at the controller terminal strip
EP (electro-pneumatic) transducer mounted above the pneumatic actuator
components.violation: EP transducers must be mounted below or at the same level as the pneumatic actuator they serve. Mounting above causes condensation from compressed air to drain down into the actuator valve body, causing corrosion, sticking, and eventual valve failure. Condensate should drain back toward the air supply where it is caught by drip legs and filters.
components.codeReference: SMACNA HVAC Controls Guidelines / Manufacturer Installation Instructions

components.name: Pneumatic tubing kinked at a 90-degree bend
components.violation: Pneumatic tubing must use proper bend fittings or gradual sweeps at direction changes. A kinked 90-degree bend restricts airflow through the tubing, causing sluggish actuator response or complete loss of pneumatic signal. The actuator may not reach full stroke, resulting in poor temperature control and comfort complaints.
components.codeReference: SMACNA HVAC Controls Guidelines — Pneumatic Tubing Installation

components.name: Sensor wire run in the same conduit as 120VAC power wiring
components.violation: Low-voltage sensor wiring (Class 2) must be run in separate conduit from line-voltage power wiring per NEC Article 725.136(A). Electromagnetic interference from the 120V power induces noise on the sensor signal, causing erratic readings and unstable control loops on the new DDC system.

explanation: Pneumatic-to-DDC conversion is a common retrofit project for IBEW/NECA controls apprentices. Union hall training emphasizes the three critical mistakes: EP transducer orientation (condensation management), pneumatic tubing handling (no kinks), and wiring separation (NEC Class 2 vs. line voltage). These conversions require understanding both legacy pneumatic systems and modern DDC to ensure the retrofit performs better than the original installation.

title: BACnet/IP Network Cable Installation

components.name: Cat6A cable used for BACnet/IP network
BACnet/IP cable run measuring 105 meters total
components.violation: TIA-568-D limits horizontal cable runs to a maximum of 100 meters (328 feet) total including patch cables. A 105-meter run exceeds this limit and will cause signal attenuation, packet loss, and intermittent communication failures on the BACnet/IP network.

components.name: Proper cable termination at RJ-45 jacks
Network switch with PoE capability for IP controllers
Cable pathway separated from power conductors
Cable tested and certified with Fluke tester

explanation: BACnet/IP runs on standard Ethernet infrastructure governed by TIA-568-D. The 100-meter maximum includes all patch cables and horizontal cabling. Exceeding this distance is a common issue in large buildings and causes unreliable BAS communication.

title: Duct Temperature Sensor Installation

components.name: Averaging temperature sensor across AHU discharge
Single-point duct temperature probe inserted only 10% into duct width
components.violation: Duct temperature probes must be inserted 25-50% of the duct width to obtain a representative reading. Inserting only 10% places the sensor in the boundary layer near the duct wall where temperature is not representative of the airstream, causing inaccurate control.

components.name: Sensor wired with shielded twisted pair cable
Sensor calibration verified at commissioning
Proprietary control system installed instead of open-protocol BACnet
components.violation: University specifications require all building automation systems to comply with ASHRAE Standard 135-2016 (BACnet). Proprietary, closed-protocol systems lock the owner into a single vendor and prevent competitive service and integration. Open-protocol BACnet is mandatory for all new installations.

components.name: All sensor points trended in BAS for commissioning verification
Sensor location accessible for future maintenance

explanation: Duct sensor insertion depth directly affects control accuracy. The boundary layer near duct walls does not represent true airstream temperature. University specs also mandate open-protocol BACnet systems to protect the owner from vendor lock-in and ensure competitive maintenance.

title: Proprietary BAS System Compliance

components.name: DDC controllers with BACnet/IP communication
Proprietary software requiring vendor-specific license for any changes
components.violation: University specifications and ASHRAE 135-2016 require that all BAS programming, graphics, and configurations be accessible using standard BACnet tools. Proprietary software that locks out the owner or third-party service providers violates the open-protocol requirement.

components.name: All points properly mapped in BACnet object database
Operator workstation with web-based interface
Trend logs configured for all critical points
Alarm notification routing configured and tested
Controller firmware not upgradable by owner without vendor involvement
components.violation: University specifications require that the building owner have full access to update, modify, and maintain all BAS components including controller firmware. Systems that require vendor involvement for routine maintenance violate the owner-access requirements of the specification.

explanation: Open-protocol BACnet compliance per ASHRAE 135-2016 is a core University requirement. Both proprietary software licensing and vendor-locked firmware prevent the owner from competitively maintaining their own building systems, which drives up lifecycle costs and reduces operational flexibility.

title: CRAC Unit iCOM Controller Setup

components.name: iCOM controller installed on CRAC-32-1 for monitoring and control
Liebert vNSA network switch installed for lead/lag coordination
CRAC-32-1 and CRAC-32-2 both set as lead units simultaneously
components.violation: Redundant CRAC units must have one designated as lead and one as lag. Setting both as lead causes both units to run at full capacity simultaneously, wasting energy and eliminating redundancy protection. The Liebert vNSA switch must coordinate lead/lag sequencing so only one unit operates at a time under normal conditions.

components.name: Temperature and humidity sensors connected to iCOM inputs
Leak detector at CRAC drip pan connected to iCOM alarm input
iCOM controller not connected to base building BMS for remote monitoring
components.violation: The high-rise commercial office requires CRAC unit controls to integrate with the base building BMS for centralized monitoring and alarm notification. An isolated iCOM controller prevents the building engineer from seeing CRAC status, alarms, or performance data from the central workstation.

explanation: CRAC unit controls in high-rise commercial TI spaces require proper lead/lag sequencing via the Liebert vNSA network switch and integration with the base building BMS. The iCOM controller manages local CRAC operation, but remote monitoring through BACnet integration is essential for facilities management per ASHRAE 135.

title: Transfer Fan Thermostat Control

components.name: Transfer fan installed to move air between zones
Wall-mounted thermostat controlling the transfer fan
Thermostat setpoint set to 90 degrees F instead of 80 degrees F
components.violation: The high-rise office specification requires transfer fan thermostats to be set at 80 degrees F (adjustable). A 90-degree setpoint means the fan will not activate until the space is dangerously overheated, defeating the purpose of the transfer fan for comfort cooling. The adjustable setpoint must start at the design value of 80 degrees F.
components.codeReference: high-rise office Mechanical Specification — Transfer Fan Controls

components.name: Thermostat wired to fan relay for on/off control
Fan relay powered from correct circuit

explanation: Transfer fans in commercial office TI spaces are controlled by wall-mounted thermostats set to activate at 80 degrees F per the high-rise office specification. The setpoint is adjustable to allow tuning, but starting at 90 degrees F provides inadequate cooling response and risks occupant discomfort in high-rise office environments.

title: Firestat and Smoke Detector Integration

components.name: Firestat installed in return air duct set to shutdown at 125 degrees F
Smoke detector installed in supply air duct
Smoke detector alarm routed only to BAS software with no hardwired shutdown
components.violation: Duct smoke detector shutdown must be hardwired directly to the HVAC equipment disconnect, independent of BAS software. A software-only alarm response means the system continues running during a smoke event if the BAS fails or the alarm is dismissed. NFPA 90A requires hardwired shutdown with audio/visual alarm at the control display panel.

components.name: Firestat hardwired to system disconnect for automatic shutdown
Audio/visual alarm indicator missing from control display panel
components.violation: The high-rise office specification requires smoke detector activation to trigger an audio/visual alarm at the control display panel in addition to system shutdown. Without the alarm indicator, building personnel may not know a smoke event occurred, delaying emergency response.
components.codeReference: NFPA 90A — Section 6.4 / high-rise office Mechanical Specification

components.name: Firestat and smoke detector test ports accessible for maintenance

explanation: The high-rise commercial project requires both firestat (>125 degrees F return air) and smoke detector integration with hardwired shutdown and audio/visual alarm at the control display panel. Life-safety functions must never rely solely on BAS software per NFPA 90A. The firestat provides thermal protection while the smoke detector provides particulate detection — both are required for comprehensive protection.

title: Motorized Outside Air Damper Interlock

components.name: Motorized damper installed on outside air intake
Damper actuator with spring-return to closed position
Motorized damper operates independently with no interlock to evaporator fan
components.violation: The high-rise office specification requires the outside air motorized damper to be interlocked with the evaporator fan operation. The damper must open only when the fan is running and close when the fan stops. Without this interlock, the damper may remain open when the fan is off, allowing unconditioned outdoor air, rain, or insects into the space and potentially causing freeze damage.
components.codeReference: IMC Section 403.2 / high-rise office Mechanical Specification

components.name: Damper sealed with gaskets for low-leakage rating
Damper position feedback wired to BMS for monitoring
No end switch to confirm damper fully open before fan starts
components.violation: An end switch or position feedback must confirm the motorized outside air damper is fully open before the evaporator fan is allowed to start. Starting the fan with the damper closed creates high negative pressure in the ductwork and starves the unit of ventilation air, potentially damaging ductwork and causing inadequate outdoor air delivery.

explanation: The high-rise commercial office requires motorized outside air dampers to be interlocked with evaporator fan operation. The damper must open before the fan starts and close when the fan stops. This interlock protects the space from uncontrolled outdoor air infiltration and prevents ductwork damage from operating against a closed damper.

title: LEED Commissioning Controls Verification

components.name: DDC controllers programmed with sequences of operation
BTU meters installed per base building standard and tied to BMS
Title 24 lighting controls installed with occupancy sensors
No independent commissioning agent verifying system performance
components.violation: Enhanced LEED commissioning requires an independent commissioning authority (CxA) — not the installing contractor — to verify all systems meet design criteria. The CxA must review submittals, witness testing, and document that systems perform per the Owner's Project Requirements (OPR). Without independent verification, LEED commissioning credits cannot be claimed.

components.name: VAV system operating with outdoor air reset enabled
Trend data not configured for post-occupancy performance verification
components.violation: Enhanced LEED commissioning requires post-occupancy trend data collection to verify systems continue to operate as designed after the building is occupied. Without trend data, there is no evidence that control sequences are performing correctly under actual load conditions. A minimum of 10 months of seasonal performance data is required.
components.codeReference: LEED v4 EAc1 — Enhanced Commissioning / ASHRAE Guideline 36

components.name: Motorized shades integrated with BMS for daylight harvesting

explanation: The high-rise commercial office requires enhanced LEED commissioning, which goes beyond standard commissioning by requiring an independent commissioning authority and post-occupancy performance verification. BTU meters tied to the BMS, lighting controls per Title 24, motorized shades, and trend data collection are all part of the integrated systems that must be verified to meet the Owner's Project Requirements per ASHRAE Guideline 0.
